sentences.text: Yeah.
And one thing I want to say about Kristallnacht, I had an uncle in Lehrberg who also was arrested.
He was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] over 55.
I don't know exactly how old.
And he was in prison, and questioned for some reason or another, for three days.
He came out, was released by the Gestapo, went home and committed suicide.
He hung himself.
He was a bachelor.
Before that, that uncle I mentioned, who volunteered for military service through World War I-- twice-- and was wounded.
And he was decorated.
It was his Germany.
He was a German all the way over.
And when, in 1937, he finally was brought to realize that he wasn't a German, and it wasn't his Germany at all, he just couldn't take it.
And he committed suicide.
Yeah, one was a brother of my father's, the first one.
Siegfried Selling.
And the other one was a brother of my mother.
His name was Huckel Laub.
Yeah.
There were quite a few suicides at that time.
People who couldn't face it.
Yes, we were put on a truck and shipped to Lehrberg, as I said.
There that prison was full and crowded.
So they didn't even have room for us anymore.
So they put us in the gym, threw in some mattresses, some honey buckets-- not enough-- and just kept us there.
Totally, about 100 to begin with.
Most of them were from Lehrberg, Furth, and the facilities there.
Because evidently, Dachau was  overcrowded and they couldn't take too many at a time.

One of reasons the prison was overcrowded is they had about 400 Sudetendeutsche or Czechs from the Sudetenland-- Germans who did not like the idea that Hitler moved into Czechoslovakia, and they were imprisoned.
At that time the prisons were full of Germans who were liberated by their friends from their homeland.
And that was quite a phenomenon, which was unexpected here.
Those people expected Hitler to march in and liberate them from Czech oppression.
But the people who marched in was the SS, who had a list of everybody who said something nasty about them and arrested them.
Yes, they were just Jews.
We were left alone as long as-- the guards were old police people.
They were quite sympathetic.
They did their duty, and nothing more.
And so finally, when we dwindled, and as they started releasing people, after we were about 20 or so, 30, we were put into cells.
And then they started to release it.
First, I was in the cell with somebody else.
Then I was alone.
And when we were dwindled down to nine, eight were shipped to Dachau.
And one was released that day.
That was the 22nd of December, 1938.
The whole action was not meant to be permanent.
It just was meant, from what I can see, it was as a shock treatment and a warning to everybody else.
We can get nasty, as if they needed that.
So they did it slowly and with as much difficulty as possible.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so when we were eight, we were shipped to Dachau.
And in the same train, the same railroad car even, there were 90 people from Czechoslovakia, Sudetenland, who were shipped there, too.
So how did they decide who was going to be released and who was going to stay?
I don't know.
Largely age, to personal feelings of the local Gestapo, and other considerations.
No, for what?
They just wanted to show for some retribution.
As you are aware of, it was supposed to be in retribution of a murder committed in Paris by a Jew against a Nazi official a week earlier.
Vom Rath.
They were by a-- said at that time.
And to answer your question, it was a  Jewish fellow named Grynszpan.
And he murdered the counselor of the German legacy in Paris, vom Rath.
The rumors were that it was-- [INAUDIBLE] said vom Rath was-- meant it for-- was meant for elimination.
And they set it up, used it as a way to find an excuse for Kristallnacht.
No, he was released earlier, on December the 4th.
I knew from prison-- that-- and one of them, we became very friendly.
His name was Ernst Thingfelder.
Thingfelder.
Yeah, he came from Furth.
He was a bachelor at that time.
Ernst.
I think we knew where we were going, yeah.
It was about four hours.
On the train it was all right.
It started getting bad after we got off the train.
Actually, we were in a-- a long distance train car.
It was a better car, better railroad car.
But as we got to Dachau, where we got out of the train, things changed.
We saw a bunch of SS guards with bayonets and loaded rifles.
They asked us to get out and chased us on a coal car.
There was a local train, which was used to bring coal to Dachau.
And they made us jump up there and helped us, so-called, with their bayonets a little bit to get up.
So we eight Jews from Lehrberg were separated.
And it was almost impossible to get on top of that car.
So, I being the youngest and almost the strongest I would say, helped the others up.
And I had a hard time stocking up myself.
So we were pushed into the camp, we're told to jump off.
They had a big shower there in the processing room.
We were-- at one end we were stripped all the way down, got a medically-- so-called medically, whatever it is-- got a shower.
Actually it was the other way around.
And got prison clothes.
And everybody grabbed the biggest shoes they could.
So when it was my turn, I had a big foot, there was no shoe for me.
So another older prisoner, who was a trustee, brought me a pair of sandals made out of rubber for a tire, from old car tires.
And that's what I wore for about a week or two, till I finally got boots.
I heard about it.
I actually heard about it quite early.
In 1933, in April, a second cousin of mine, who was a lawyer in Munich, was arrested and sent to Dachau.
And he died just three months later.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we knew about it.
Yes, to get us into camp.
It was an open one, one of those open [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] railroad cars.
No, no.
It was an open car, a flatbed.
There was no place to sit.
It was an open car.
No, then they pulled us with a rickety engine into the camp.
From the railroad station to the camp.
Yeah, when we were in the camp.
Then we were into the processing room, where we were stripped.
No, but another interesting thing in that process.
I mentioned I had a friend there.
He was shipped with me to Dachau.
He was very religious.
Ernst Thingfelder.
And he wanted to bring his tefellin into the camp.
We dissuaded him.
They probably would have killed him if he would have tried to take them along.
So that really was a scene do get him to leave his tallit and tefellin behind.
There with all the rest of our clothes.
They were just pushed in a bag.
And when we got out we got that bag back.
There was-- and all we had, what we could take along-- we were stark naked-- was whatever we could put it our handkerchief and carry alongside, like a little bit of money and--
So-called, they saw whether we still breathed, were breathing.
And we did, evidently.
Yes.
That means I was the 31,000th person.
Whoever went to Dachau, they went by numbers.
The first one who came to Dachau from the Kristallnacht, the numbering started in 18800.
And so there were about 12,000 Jews from the Kristallnacht in Dachau.
It was on the left breast.
On the right breast.
On the right breast, sorry.
On the clothing, yeah.
It was a prison striped blue and white.
It was winter and they didn't have enough prison uniforms.
In Dachau they had summer uniforms and winter uniforms.
So we got the summer uniforms.
It was in December.
We were pushed to a barracks, which they had.
And we had in double time.
Everything was moving in double time.
And the guards rode along on bicycles.
We were not allowed to walk-- we had to run all the time.
So about midnight, we got into that little room in the barracks, which was crowded wall to wall with prisoners.
How many people would you say were inside the barracks?
About 200 in this particular room.
It was called a block.
It consisted of four, let's say, apartments with a living room and a bedroom, so to speak.
It was supposed to hold 50 people, 50 beds.
But before we came, long before, it was already in July from what I understand, they started to prepare for that.
And they put wooden boards along the walls and the shelves.
And we had some straw.
And that just was it.
Yes.
It was block eight, room four.
I don't think I could do justice of this-- I mean, I may not even attempt to do it.
That has been documented someplace.
And everybody can find out what it actually was.
So better than I ever could tell.
It was dreary, cold, wet, and just like a prison [INAUDIBLE] look.
Oh, definitely.
Two rows.
Inside the barracks they were good.
It had a wash room, which had to be kept clean.
And they had toilets, water-- toilets-- which had to be kept clean.
Actually, they were so clean that a lot of punishment resulted from not being clean enough.
Be beaten up.
Of course.
For nothing.
I don't know.
Because I was in the way.
Yeah.
The guards, SS guards.
There was-- they had trustees, prisoners who are in charge of the block, and one of each room.
And they couldn't do much anyway.
They were said to-- they had to make sure that we kept the place clean, that we did everything we should do.
Otherwise they would get punished.
And being a trustee, by its nature, was a little cushier than the rest of the prison life.
So they would not risk losing that job if we fall down.
So they made sure we toed the line.
Yeah.
Yes.
But let me wait to the end when I make my comments.
And I would like to describe that situation by itself.
For it will lead to my conclusions.
Yeah.
We didn't have any job, regular jobs, which is hard labor.
We were not put in hard labor because they just didn't have enough facilities.
So we had-- everybody had to be out of the barracks in the morning after breakfast.
So we either stood around freezing at the parade grounds, or we ran around to get warm-- of course in groups, not individually.
And it was-- we was-- we were exercised.
Running around.
And quite a few collapsed from the rigor of the weather and the conditions.
Yes, definitely.
Roll call was twice a day, in the morning at 5 o' clock, and in the evening at 6:00.
And during one of the roll calls, towards the end of my stay, one prisoner had escaped.
And the commandant said, you stay here and-- and stand here till that prisoner's been recaptured.
So we were standing there all night at attention.
And some of us fell down and couldn't stay at attention.
Since there were several thousand people, those more or less in the middle had it a little easier.
I must have dozed off, and I suddenly I was still on my feet.
I was hit in the back with a rifle butt.
Luckily for me, I did not hit the ground.
I could catch myself and got up.
Or they would have killed me right then and there.
That morning, at 6 o'clock after sunrise, we were allowed one hour to go back and have breakfast.
The place was littered with people who had collapsed-- quite a few.
That is they were all dead.
He was recaptured the next evening.
And I remember when he was brought back to the camp.
And then we finally were allowed to go back to our barracks.
I'm sure he died-- not easily.
Swill.
Swill, what you feed the pigs.
We had every three days, two shared a loaf of bread.
And three times a day we had to go to the kitchen, got big buckets of coffee and mostly tapioca.
And in the evening it was stew, as I described already before.
So, talking about what we were fed.
That friend of mine, Ernst Thingfelder, he was strictly orthodox kosher.
He recoiled of eating that stews, which was everything together.
So I gave him most of my bread, and I ate the meat-- for he wouldn't touch the meat.
So I practically didn't eat any bread there.
And we both got out.
Were you in the barracks with him, the same barracks?
We were in the same barracks.
We started out with 200.
Originally the barracks held 300.
But as I said-- I'm talking about our room alone.
If you want to talk about the barracks, you multiply it by four.
We were pushed in, as I said.
And later as more and more were released, it was just at the day when I was released, they started to reconvert the barracks to the original bunks, which held 52 people per room.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Before that, we actually dwindled down from 200 to 100 and more.
We were lucky, we had a few more blankets.
We had a little more room.
We didn't have to sleep practically on top of each other.
We didn't talk.
Lights out and you shut up.
The big question was, why?
And I compared it, being a history buff-- even at that young age, had read a lot of history, which included the crusades, the inquisition, all that stuff.
I said, what is the difference of it?
What's the difference between-- and to put it very frankly, between suffering ad majorem Dei glorium, which is the slogan of the Jesuits, or just for the hell of it?
I couldn't find any difference.
Ad majorem Dei glorium.
That means for the greater glory of God.
That is the slogan of the Jesuits.
And just for the hell of it is what we went through.
And I couldn't see much difference.
And that is all the same God.
Lots of people came out of Dachau very religious.
I think more of them-- and I was one of them-- without religion.
That's where I really stopped believing, if I ever believed much.
But I continued to go through the motions.
Oh, no way.
Nothing like that.
No.
Actually, it's enough.
But we were talking about food.

There was within the prison of Dachau, there was a compound with the barbed wire, with three barracks separated, which was reserved-- or it wasn't reserved-- which was the compound for the Jehovah's Witnesses.
They were there because they refused to swear allegiance to the flag, or to the country or whatever else it is.
And they were kept there till they got up and swore allegiance.
They just didn't.
They were the only cohesive groups there who was a little bit-- who was concerned about their fellow prisoners.
And one of them was caught sharing his bread with somebody outside that particular compound.
So the commandant ruled, evidently we feed you too much.
Instead of half a loaf, you get only a third of a loaf.
That was three men to a loaf of bread.
And since they believed in the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the sabbath, or Sunday, as a day of rest, they were made to work seven days a week.
Usually labor in Dachau was six days a week.
But they had-- they was all seven days.
